title: Dog Panting Heavily While Resting: Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog panting while resting? Learn why heavy breathing at rest is a high-priority emergency and how to triage the situation for your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Heavy panting while resting is a serious sign of respiratory distress or pain and requires immediate veterinary evaluation. If your pet has pale or blue gums, treat it as a life-threatening emergency and contact a vet immediately.

What does it mean if my dog is panting heavily while resting?

  • Panting is a natural cooling mechanism after exercise, but it is abnormal if your dog is resting in a cool environment.
  • Abnormal panting suggests the body is working overtime to compensate for a lack of oxygen or internal pain.
  • This behavior often indicates a systemic health issue that requires professional medical attention.

Is heavy panting at rest a veterinary emergency?

Urgency Level: High.

  • Heavy panting at rest is frequently a sign of respiratory distress, congestive heart failure, heatstroke, or severe internal pain.
  • It is a life-threatening emergency if the panting is accompanied by blue, purple, or extremely pale gums.
  • Using abdominal muscles to breathe is a critical sign that your dog needs immediate care.
  • Contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately if you observe these symptoms.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my dog's heavy panting?

  • Providing a 10-15 second video of the breathing pattern helps the triage team visualize the effort, rhythm, and depth of breaths.
  • Seeing whether the chest or the abdomen is doing the work helps the medical team prioritize your pet's care.
  • Visual evidence allows the clinic to prepare the necessary equipment before you even walk through the door.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Panting heavily while resting can indicate life-threatening conditions requiring emergency intervention. Potential underlying causes include upper airway obstruction (characterized by noisy breathing such as stridor or stertor), alveolar flooding secondary to pulmonary edema, severe bronchoconstriction, or brain-stem pathology affecting ventilation. Circulatory compromise, such as shock, cardiopulmonary arrest, extreme arrhythmias, cardiac tamponade, or acute intravascular volume loss due to hemorrhage should also be considered. Profound hypo- or hyperthermia can also present acutely. General anesthesia may be indicated in severely fatigued and dyspneic animals to facilitate oxygen administration, esophageal drainage, and upper respiratory tract suction, allowing for muscle rest and overcoming primary muscle fatigue. Mechanical or manual ventilation may be required, but should be carefully assessed due to potential delays in recovery, especially in brachycephalic breeds.

Chapter: Emergency, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1662)

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause resting panting?
faqs.answer: While anxiety can cause panting, it is crucial to rule out medical emergencies first. If the panting is sudden and heavy, treat it as a physical health issue until a vet confirms otherwise.

faqs.question: What should I check on my dog right now?
faqs.answer: Check the color of their gums; they should be bubblegum pink. Also, check if they are standing with their elbows out or neck extended, as these are signs of struggling to breathe.

faqs.question: Should I wait to see if it stops?
faqs.answer: No. Respiratory distress can escalate quickly. It is always safer to have a professional assessment when a dog exhibits heavy breathing at rest.
